1: al tira ve'al techat: ‘do not fear or be dismayed’ — YHWH’s opening words to Joshua are the exact formula of the original commission (Joshua 1:9). The repetition is deliberate: after the defeat at Ai and the Achan crisis, Joshua needs the commission renewed. Every new task in Joshua receives this divine declaration; the formula is not a one-time grant but a renewable resource. The same formula reaches its NT form in John 14:27: “let not your hearts be troubled, neither let them be afraid.”
2: raq shallah uvehamatah tavzu lachem: ‘only their plunder and livestock you shall take for yourselves’ — the key difference from Jericho: Ai is not cherem. The livestock and goods may be kept. This is YHWH’s liberality after judgment: Jericho was the firstfruits city requiring total consecration; subsequent cities allow Israel to benefit. The first belongs entirely to YHWH; subsequent gifts may be used by the recipient.
3: Joshua chose capable warriors for the night ambush. The precision of the ambush strategy contrasts sharply with the cavalier two-to-three-thousand approach of the first Ai expedition (Joshua 7:3-4). After Jericho and Achan, Joshua does not economize on military preparation. The strategy is explicitly commanded by YHWH (v2: “set an ambush”) — not improvised but divinely prescribed. Divine guidance does not replace careful planning but directs it.
4: u'atem ta'arvu le'ir: ‘set an ambush against the city’ — erev (ambush/lying in wait) in holy war is a legitimate instrument. The contrast with Jericho is complete: there, the conquest required seven days of public liturgical marching; here, it requires careful concealment and tactical surprise. The God who prescribed the Jericho liturgy also prescribes the Ai ambush — both are divine strategies.
5: Joshua’s tactical explanation: they will advance toward Ai provoking the same response as before. The word yaratzu (they will run out) predicts the enemy’s response precisely — not just tactical prediction but covenantal certainty: YHWH has said he will hand Ai over; the tactical plan operates within that certainty. Military planning and divine promise are not competing categories.
6: ki yomeru nasoh nivchal milefaneihem: ‘for they will say, “They are fleeing before us, as before”’ — the planned feigned retreat exploits Ai’s memory of their previous victory. The king of Ai will repeat his successful strategy without recognizing that circumstances have changed. Those who have defeated God’s people once become overconfident, and their overconfidence becomes the means of their destruction: Pharaoh pursued Israel into the Red Sea on the strength of his previous success.
7: veatem taqumu min hama'arav: ‘then you shall rise from the ambush and seize the city’ — the rising of the ambush force is timed to the moment when Ai’s army is committed and cannot retreat. The ambush must hold still until the right moment; premature action would ruin the trap. Patience in waiting and decisiveness in acting are both required.
8: ketsav et hair baesh ki dabar YHWH tasunu: ‘set the city on fire — do this according to the word of YHWH’ — the fire is YHWH’s command, not tactical improvisation. The burning of Ai is an act of covenantal judgment, not military vandalism.
9: The ambush force positioned itself between Bethel and Ai to the west. The specific geographic reference locates the action precisely in the central hill country. The ambush waits through the night — another discipline of delayed action: the force that will seize the city must wait in concealment until the tactical moment arrives. Premature action would have been as fatal as Achan’s premature taking.
10: vayashkem Yehoshua baboqer: early rising again (cf. Joshua 6:12; Joshua 7:16) — the consistent pattern of early morning readiness for divine action. Joshua reviewed the troops (vayipqod, he mustered them) then led the elders of Israel at the head of the army: the covenant leadership goes to battle together, not from the rear.
11: The main army took up position north of Ai with a valley between them and the city. The tactical geography is precise: the valley makes a direct assault obvious while requiring Ai’s army to cross it to engage. The narrative attention to geography in chapters 6-12 is characteristic: the land is not a generic backdrop but a specific, named, remembered territory whose geography is woven into the memory of divine acts.
12: Five thousand men placed in ambush between Bethel and Ai to the west — a second ambush unit. The west/east positioning creates a pincer: the main army draws Ai eastward while the ambush takes the city from the west. The coordination required speaks to the discipline of the conquest force after the Achan crisis.
13: The positioning is complete: main force north, ambush west. Joshua went that night into the valley between the forces — commanders’ presence in the operational zone before the battle. Leadership is proximate to risk, not insulated from it.
14: vayashkem melech haAi vayimalaru vayetze: ‘when the king of Ai saw them, he hurried and went out’ — the verb yishkimu (they rose early, same root as Joshua’s early rising) is pointed: Ai’s king rises early to meet the threat, mirroring Joshua’s early rising for obedience. The enemy is energetic and responsive — but his energy is deployed in the wrong direction because he does not know the ambush is behind him. Asymmetric knowledge of the full situation is what Joshua has and the king of Ai lacks.
15: vayitnakkel Yehoshua vekol Yisrael lifneihem: ‘Joshua and all Israel made themselves appear beaten before them’ — vayitnakkel is reflexive: they made themselves appear beaten. Deliberate performance, a tactical fiction in the service of YHWH’s strategy. The tradition regards tactical stratagems as categorically different from prohibited deception; the Rahab narrative handled deception in service of YHWH’s purposes without moral condemnation.
16: vayizaqeu kol haam asher bair lirdof achareihem: ‘all the people who were in the city were called out to pursue them’ — the complete emptying of Ai is the trap springing shut. The city is evacuated of its defenders; the ambush will walk into an undefended position. The king’s initial success (routing the feint) becomes the mechanism of his total defeat.
17: velo nish'ar ish beAi uveVeit El asher lo yatza acharei Yisrael: ‘not a man was left in Ai or Bethel who had not gone out after Israel’ — Bethel’s troops also committed to the pursuit. Total commitment to the wrong direction is total vulnerability to the right one. Forces totally committed to pursuing apparent advantage are exposed to the power that works precisely through what appears defeated.
18: nteh bakidon asher beyadechu el haAi: ‘point the javelin in your hand toward Ai’ — the extended spear deliberately echoes Moses’s extended staff at Rephidim (Exodus 17:11-12): “whenever Moses held up his hand, Israel prevailed.” Joshua’s kidon (javelin) points toward Ai as Moses’s staff pointed toward the battle. Both gestures are not magical but covenantal — visible expressions of dependence on YHWH’s agency in the battle.
19: vayaqumu hama'arav bimheram: ‘the ambush rose quickly from their position’ — the force that waited through the night in disciplined stillness now acts with maximum speed (bimheram). Patience in waiting and decisiveness in acting are both required; the one who cannot hold still is as dangerous as the one who acts too late.
20: vayyipenu anshei haAi achareihem vayyiru vehinneh alah ashan hair hashamayyim: ‘the men of Ai looked back and saw the smoke from the city rising to the sky’ — the smoke is the visible sign that everything has gone wrong simultaneously: the city is captured behind them, they are committed too far forward to retreat, the feigned retreat is wheeling around to become an actual attack. Judgment often works this way: the moment of apparent escape is the moment the trap is most completely closed.
21: vayar Yehoshua vekol Yisrael ki lakad hama'arav et hair: ‘Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had captured the city’ — the smoke visible to both sides works differently on each: for Israel, the signal to turn and fight; for Ai, the signal that all is lost. The same event produces opposite responses depending on which side one is on. This is the consistent biblical pattern: divine acts are simultaneously redemptive and judgmental, life and death (2 Corinthians 2:15-16).
22: vayihu Yisrael betoch: ‘Israel was in their midst’ — the double encirclement is complete: main army attacking from the front and sides, ambush attacking from the rear, the men of the city trapped between. The word betoch (in the midst of) is the same used of YHWH’s presence among Israel; the reversal is complete — Israel was surrounded and routed at Joshua 7:5; now Ai is surrounded and destroyed.
23: ve et melech haAi tafsu chai: ‘they captured the king of Ai alive’ — the single exception to total destruction: the king is taken alive for public execution by Joshua (v29). The king’s public death before all Israel is both military demonstration and theological statement: the one who organized resistance to YHWH’s purpose is publicly judged, not merely eliminated.
24: vayyishvu kol Yisrael el haAi vayyakohu leftzi chariv: ‘all Israel returned to Ai and struck it with the edge of the sword’ — the systematic completion of the battle follows the ambush’s seizure of the city. What the first expedition failed to achieve through presumption is accomplished through obedience, divine strategy, and patient execution.
25: kol hanoflim bayom hahu me'ish ve'ad ishah shemeim ashar alef: ‘twelve thousand men and women fell that day — all the people of Ai’ — the death toll at Ai (12,000) far exceeds the initial Israelite defeat (36 dead, Joshua 7:5). The scale of the reversal is precise. The number twelve thousand — twelve tribes, thousands as the unit of military organization — may carry symbolic weight as representing a full tribal force.
26: veYehoshua lo hashiv yado asher natah bakidon ad asher hechrim et kol yoshvei haAi: ‘Joshua did not draw back his hand… until he had devoted all the inhabitants of Ai to destruction’ — the sustained extended arm is the explicit echo of Moses at Rephidim (Exodus 17:12: “his hands were steady until the going down of the sun”). Moses held up his hands throughout the battle with Amalek; Joshua holds his spear extended throughout the battle with Ai. The sustained physical posture of the leader signifying sustained divine authority is a deliberate typological parallel: Joshua inheriting Moses’s role enacted through the same bodily posture.
27: raq habehemah ushlal hair hazot bazazu lahem Yisrael kadvar YHWH: ‘only the livestock and plunder of Ai Israel took for themselves — as YHWH commanded Joshua’ — the explicit contrast with Jericho: the same act that was cherem-violation at Jericho is covenant-obedience at Ai. The moral valence of an action depends not only on the action itself but on whether YHWH has permitted or prohibited it. Achan took what YHWH had kept sacred; Israel takes at Ai what YHWH has released.
28: vayyisrof Yehoshua et haAi vaysiymehah tel olam shemamah: ‘Joshua burned Ai and made it a tel olam — a permanent ruin’ — tel olam (forever-mound) is the origin of the archaeological term tell: a mound of accumulated ruins marking a destroyed city. The word olam (forever) makes the destruction permanent and deliberate; Ai will not be rebuilt for ordinary habitation. The destroyed cities leave permanent marks in the terrain as ongoing testimonies to the conquest.
29: ve et melech haAi talah al ha-etz: ‘the king of Ai he hanged on a tree’ — execution by hanging invokes Deuteronomy 21:23: “a hanged man is cursed by God.” The king dies under the covenant curse. The sunset-burial requirement (Deuteronomy 21:23) is observed. Paul applies the Deuteronomy 21:23 curse-formula to Christ’s death (Galatians 3:13: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, ‘Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree’”). The king of Ai died as a cursed man; Christ died as the one who became the curse for others.
30: az yivneh Yehoshua mizbeach laYHWH Elohei Yisrael behar Eval: ‘then Joshua built an altar to YHWH God of Israel on Mount Ebal’ — the altar-building on Ebal immediately follows the conquest of Ai, before the conquest of the southern or northern territories. The sequence is deliberate: conquest leads to covenant renewal, not to further conquest. The altar on Ebal was commanded by Moses (Deuteronomy 27:4-8) as the first act upon entering the land.
31: kazasher tzivah Moshe eved YHWH livnei Yisrael kakatuv besefer torat Moshe: ‘as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded… as written in the Book of the Law of Moses’ — the altar is built exactly as specified: unhewn stones, no iron tool (cf. Exodus 20:25: hewn stone would impose human craft on what is offered to YHWH). The altar’s simplicity is theological: YHWH does not require human artistry to make his altar acceptable. Contrast with the elaborate metalwork of the tabernacle; the Ebal altar is deliberate simplicity for a specific purpose.
32: vayiktov sham al ha'avanim et mishneh torat Moshe: ‘he wrote on the stones a copy of the Law of Moses’ — the Torah inscribed on stones at the moment of land-entry creates a visible, permanent record of the covenant obligation governing the land’s use. The inscribed Torah on Ebal’s stones anticipates the new covenant’s inscription: “I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts” (Jeremiah 31:33).
33: vehager: ‘and the resident alien’ — the covenant assembly at Ebal and Gerizim explicitly includes the ger (resident alien) alongside the native-born Israelite. The inclusion of sojourners in the covenant assembly is remarkable: the Sinai covenant was given to Israel, but at the first renewal ceremony in the land, non-Israelite residents are included. The universality latent in the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 12:3) begins to manifest at Ebal: the covenant community is expanding, not contracting, in the land.
34: veachar qara et kol divrei hatorah habrachah vehaqelalah: ‘afterward he read all the words of the Law, the blessings and the curses’ — the full public reading of Deut 27-28 in the land, with the entire community assembled, creates a formal covenant ratification. The blessings and curses are read aloud before all Israel: the community enters the land as a covenant community fully informed of its obligations and consequences. The transparency of the covenant terms — public, inclusive, comprehensive — means no one can claim ignorance of what was at stake.
35: lo hayah davar mikol asher tzivah Moshe asher lo qara Yehoshua: ‘there was not a word of all that Moses commanded that Joshua did not read before the assembly’ — total comprehensiveness: every word, nothing omitted. Women, little children, and resident aliens were all included — every member of the community heard the complete Torah. The fullness-of-revelation principle: the covenant community receives the full disclosure of what God requires and what God promises. The church’s obligation is the same: “the whole counsel of God” (Acts 20:27), not a curated selection.
